title: Google I/O 2025 – KXKP2tDPW4Y
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] JAMAL EASON: Hi,
welcome to What's New in Android
Development Tools. I'm Jamal, Director of Product
Management on the Android Development Experience team. TOR NORBYTE: And I'm Tor. I'm the Engineering Director
for Android Developer Tools and Libraries. [APPLAUSE, CHEERING] JAMAL EASON: So
together, we're going to walk through some updates
to Android Studio, where our mission is to make it easy
for you to make high quality apps for Android across a whole
portfolio of Android devices. First up, I'll cover a quick
update to the product roadmap, and then Tor will give a
demo of some of the latest features and action. And lastly, I'll wrap up and
give some additional feature updates. OK, first up, let's take a
quick peek at the roadmap. Now, as a reminder, we
have doubled our releases of Android Studio where the
initial platform release is really focused on getting
Android Studio aligned to the latest version of
the intelligent platform. Plus, it includes a handful of
bugs and product quality fixes. Then, we follow up
with a feature drop release, which includes
Android-specific features. So since I/O last year, we
went through a whole release cycle of Android Studio
Ladybug and Meerkat. So let me walk
through a few updates that you might have missed. For Android Studio Ladybug
feature drop release, we have a handful of features
around Wear OS Preview & Health Services. Plus, for those of you who use
ads in your apps and games, we improve the App
Links Assistant to make it even easier
to test your app links. And we integrated Google Play
SDK Insights right into the IDE, so you don't have to go
to the Play Dev Console to see potential
publishing hurdles. Lastly, we spent a
notable amount of time on addressing over
700 bugs, both reported by you and our
internal quality team. For the Android Studio
Meerkat release, we are continuing to invest in
more refined tools, such as, Jetpack Compose Preview
Enhancements, and a new template for adding Kotlin Multiplatform
to existing projects.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KXKP2tDPW4Y
text:
For the Android Studio
Meerkat release, we are continuing to invest in
more refined tools, such as, Jetpack Compose Preview
Enhancements, and a new template for adding Kotlin Multiplatform
to existing projects. Plus, we also updated
the Build Menu to be more efficient and more
streamlined in this release. And across all these
releases, we've been building on
our capabilities of AI-powered Developer
Assistance with Gemini and Android Studio. From code suggestions,
generating documentation, to solving crashes, we've
applied Gemini and Android Studio to a wide range of
your core developer workflows. Now, with AI, we know
this is an evolving space with almost every couple
of weeks a new improvement to the Gemini model. Our philosophy with AI is that
it's not a one size fits all. Let me explain. So in some cases,
we know you want to go from a prototype,
a new idea on our API. It needs to go from zero to 1
and rapidly think of a new idea. While on the other hand, you
have an existing code base that you need help with
taking your idea from one to production, a production
environment where you have your core app business,
where you probably have a higher bar for
quality and stability. So for the Android
Studio team we have what we're delivering on
a wide range of features that address all these mindsets. Today, we'll show you some of
the latest AI-enabled features we've been working on in
Android Studio Narwhal, plus some other surprises. And in addition to AI work, we
show you some new improvements in other critical workflows. OK, now, I have Tor to walk us
through some of the new features in Android Studio. And after we finish
the demo, I'll walk through some
additional product updates. OK, Tor, take it away. TOR NORBYTE: All right. Good morning, everybody. So I have a lot to show. [APPLAUSE, CHEERING] Thanks. And I'm actually going to start
with AI features this time. But before I get into some
of the bigger features, I just want to highlight some
of the usability improvements
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KXKP2tDPW4Y
text:
So I have a lot to show. [APPLAUSE, CHEERING] Thanks. And I'm actually going to start
with AI features this time. But before I get into some
of the bigger features, I just want to highlight some
of the usability improvements we made, because I just think
it makes it nicer to use. So, yes, you can see
the screen already. So the first one I'll talk
about is code completion. So we're now doing syntax
highlighting on the ghost text. So you can see it's subtle,
but it's sort of translucent. And I think it just makes it
a lot easier to visually scan. And in chat, we've rewritten
the entire thing in Compose. So we now have nice
animations and motion. When I run a query here, you
can see I can interrupt it, another nice animation. We are now streaming
code snippets so they appear more immediately,
and the scrolling behavior is better. So you don't have to
scroll back up to see the beginning of the reply. We also have a
context drawer, so you can see which files
are implicitly attached to your queries. And you can also go and
attach your own files if you want to ask
about an image, for example, to
convert it to code. And notice how we're now able
to actually use Gemini 2.5 Pro. So we're going to start
showing the model you're using in the bottom right corner. So let me just say,
silly question, hello. Look at the model selector
when I press Hello. See the nice shimmer? I like small stuff like that. All right. Anyway, Gemini 2.5
Pro is enabling us to write some much more
advanced features than before. And so the first feature I
want to show you like that is our update assistant. So we know that library
updates are a pain. We've heard that loud
and clear, especially, when there are source-breaking
changes, which there often are. So the Upgrade
Assistant is basically trying to automate that for you. So here we have a TOML file. We can see lots of
dependency warnings. And a new improvement in
the latest version of lint is that it doesn't just
check the maven.google.com
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KXKP2tDPW4Y
text:
trying to automate that for you. So here we have a TOML file. We can see lots of
dependency warnings. And a new improvement in
the latest version of lint is that it doesn't just
check the maven.google.com dependencies, it checks, well,
for example, Maven Central, too. So we can see we have a
new version of Kotlin. So now, I can apply a quick
fix, update all libraries with Gemini. When I do that, we are
analyzing the build files. You can see it's now proposing
to update the Compiler SDK version, AGP, Gradle itself. Here, we have a
bill of materials and it's showing
us which version updates within that
bill of materials is going to be updated. All the blue libraries
here are hyperlinks. That means that we found
release notes for these updates that we're going to
augment the agent with. And at the end, I can
let it do its thing. So I'm going to
press start update. And so now, the agent is
going to build the project. We've already changed
the build files. You can see in the commit
window here that some files have already been changed. Now, it's running a build. It's found an error. It's now going to
reason about that error. And there it is. You can see the explanation for
what it's doing along the way. It's reading the file. And if you went and saw the
developer keynote yesterday, you'll notice this
looks a bit different. So our original update bot-- update agent, sorry, was
written for Gemini 2.0 and we had it months ago. But then 2.5 has
enabled us to write a much more advanced agent. So this isn't a facelift,
this is a body lift. We're now putting the update
agent into the generic agent instead, and it's kind of fun. So you can see it
has now succeeded. It's running a Gradle sync, and
it should be done momentarily, I would think. There it is. And then we get a
summary at the end where I can review the changes. So this was a deliberately,
very simple update so that we could sit and watch it live. What I want to do now is start
a longer and, frankly, harder update job, and run
it in the background
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KXKP2tDPW4Y
text:
where I can review the changes. So this was a deliberately,
very simple update so that we could sit and watch it live. What I want to do now is start
a longer and, frankly, harder update job, and run
it in the background during the rest of the demo. So for that, I'm going to use
Android Studio in the Cloud. So here is Firebase Studio. And I can open up Android
Studio Workspaces here. And I've already started one. So what you're seeing
here is a version of Studio running in the Cloud. I've preloaded the
Sunflower Project, but I've gone back in time. I've checked out an older
version of this project, and I'm going to just see if
the AI can update it for us. By the way, we have implemented
backup and sync now. So you can see that all my
settings from my local computer and all my remote
instances can be synced with my Google account,
as well as my JetBrains account. All right, let's kick
off the update here. And I've run this update about
10 times in the last week, and it's succeeded in
about half of them. So it's truly a
difficult project. So we'll see what it does. Start update. All right. So it's off to the races. We'll check back in on
this in a little bit. So updating libraries is toil. It's not something anyone
really enjoys, I think. And I'll wager many of you
feel the same way about writing integration tests. Certainly, I feel that way. Unit tests, great, but
integration tests not so much. So the next feature
I want to show you is meant to help with that. So historically, you test
these things using Espresso. But we're now launching
journeys where you get to describe
a user journey, which is a critical task
the user is trying to do in natural language. So here, we have a journey. This is a journey file. And this is actually just
XML behind the scenes. So you can do code
reviews on this. You can check it
into version control. You can even comment out parts,
like I've done here temporarily. But we also give you a
nice graphical editor so I can drag and drop
to reorder these things.

So you can do code
reviews on this. You can check it
into version control. You can even comment out parts,

to reorder these things. I can add new things, and I can

that we can add custom filters. So what it's doing now is
it's building the project. It's got lots of modules so it

to tap on the Filters panel. Then it's going to make sure

situation, because we don't in Studio exactly
when one task is done and when the next one begins. So we're just highlighting

actually have the right-- oh, and it's failed. That's exciting. So now, I'll show you the next
feature to help with that. So we have these results shown

Declaration of compute square. So it is using an

I'm going to turn that back

be using a custom variant, maybe you've set up a

we are teaching this agent about Gradle. So it is not making assumptions. It's not just saying, it's

these tool calls. So it's looking up
the right places. By the way, look

to create this test. And I can accept that change. I'm just going to auto

this test for me. And let's go in
and break the code. So I'm going to delete

pass, and if not, fix the code? So again, it should

running the tests. Hopefully, they will fail. Yeah, so the tests were good. They were catching
this behavior. Let me scroll to
the bottom so you can see it is now

pretty useful. [APPLAUSE] All right. It looks like I have

So this is-- yeah,
pretty useful. [APPLAUSE] All right. It looks like I have

file, and this one has a lint warning,
but no quick fix. But we can actually ask
the AI if it can fix it. Can you fix the
warnings in this file? So again, the agent
should have access to look up the warnings that
are coming in this file. You can see it's analyzed
the current file. And it is now,
basically, implemented this using a single
text view, which is with a compound
drawable, which is what lint was suggesting
it should do verbally. All right. That's the agent. I want to hit a couple
more quick AI features. So we have integration
with Crashlytics. So here, we now have a
panel, which explains what this crash is doing. And the reason this is great
is because in Studio, we have access to your source code. So this isn't trying to
hallucinate and guess based on the crash thread dump
based on the method names what might be the problem. This is actually
looking at the code. And in some cases, we're
able to then suggest a fix. So here, we have a pretty
reasonable fix for this crash. Here's a second example. So this crash, if I
jump to this crash, it can't go to the right
line because this source file has changed since
the crash happened. But in Crashlytics
and Play vitals we actually have the commit ID
corresponding to this crash. So we can account for
that line number drift when we are feeding
the AI the source file. Since you can see
the explanation here is actually correct, even though
the line numbers didn't match. One other quick
AI feature I want to show you is it's
Compose Integration. So here is a Compose composable. And I don't have
any previews for it, but I can actually press this
Auto Generate Compose preview button. This will now analyze the file
and create a preview for me with some reasonable
sample data. So when I accept the changes,
it should rebuild momentarily. Rebuilding?

but I can actually press this
Auto Generate Compose preview button. This will now analyze the file
and create a preview for me with some reasonable

nice to just have a button. All right. So I think that's

to show you is Firebase Device Streaming. So Firebase Device

to these devices, you're actually connecting to
devices in their device labs, not Google's device labs. And so doing this is easy. You just pick a device
that'll add them to this list. And I've already ahead
of time connected to this Samsung Galaxy S25. We can switch to that now. And here it is. And what I like to do whenever
I connect to a remote device is to pull up Google Maps
and see where in the world this device is located. And so let's see. And we can see it's in Korea. It's in Insa-dong. And you can also notice that the
interactivity is really good. Even though this is
halfway across the world, it's pretty fast and I can
run my app there, as well. So Firebase Device Streaming
and the new device partner labs is a great way to test and
interactively debug your apps in a variety of scenarios. Last but not least,
let's return to device so someone else can use it. Right. We've been working really

working on USB stack, and so we had
speed improvements,

title: Google I/O 2025 – KXKP2tDPW4Y
text:
Last but not least,
let's return to device so someone else can use it. Right. We've been working really
hard on device connectivity for several years. So last year we were
working on USB stack, and so we had
speed improvements, reliability improvements, and we
also shipped the USB cable speed detection. So hopefully you all have
USB 3 cables now, right? Yeah. But if you're an
engineer who doesn't like to connect your
phone with a cable, I have really good news. We've been working
on ADB Wi-Fi, again. I know, there were
many problems, but fixing it was really hard. We've been working
on the entire stack, swapping out whole pieces. We did work on
Studio, lots of work on ADB, and even on the
Android operating system. So for example, a common
problem in the past with the OS was that if your phone
went into standby mode, it was no longer listening
for connections from Studio, so they wouldn't reconnect. But now that's changed. So with the new version of
the OS, once you're paired, it'll always reconnect. We also reworked the way
the pairing flow works. Because a very common
problem when you try to pair and it wasn't working, was that
the device and Studio were not actually on the same network. So what I've done here is
I'd like to tempt the demo gods with the Wi-Fi demo. So I brought my
own Wi-Fi router. So I now have Studio. My laptop is on my own
little network Studio demo and so is my test device. And so the phone and
laptop are not paired yet. But when I turn on Wi-Fi
wireless debugging on my phone, within about a second, we
should see the device showing up in Device Manager. Yeah, here it is. You can see it, right? So they're not paired yet. [APPLAUSE] And another nice feature
is that this is actually listing the device name I've
set for myself in my phone under about phone. That requires an
operating system update, which we're working on. So on older phones, it'll
just say-- for this one, it would say Google Pixel phone. And so now, I can initiate
pairing by pressing this button.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KXKP2tDPW4Y
text:
That requires an
operating system update, which we're working on. So on older phones, it'll
just say-- for this one, it would say Google Pixel phone. And so now, I can initiate
pairing by pressing this button. Let's do it just to make
sure that it actually works. It's confused by the lights. A lot of reflection. Here we go. And they're paired. Cool. All right. So now, we can see the
phone here connected. All right. Thank you. [APPLAUSE] All right. So now, I'm going
to show you Compose. So let me switch to this layout. So when you are building
adaptive apps, or any app, really, it's very
important to make sure that your Compose layouts
are resizing correctly under a variety of
available sizes. And so we have a new
feature for that. So in the Compose Preview
window, you can switch to Focus. And so that lets you focus
on one particular preview and you can quickly
switch between them. So notice how it's pretty
subtle in dark theme. But in the bottom right
corner, there's a resize knob. And so when I press
this, I can now interactively resize
the composable and see how it behaves. Yeah. [APPLAUSE] And you can see we have these
markers on the surface, too. So I can see roughly what a
foldable should be, and so on. And then I can go ahead and
save this preview, if I want. That'll just write the size
back on the preview annotation, or I can restore. And speaking of restore, let's
talk about backup and restore. So let me run my app, again. So when users get
a new phone, they can transfer their
settings to the new phone. But it doesn't always work if
app developers aren't doing it quite right. Or worse, some app
developers opt out of it. And this is, obviously, a
bad experience for users. So we're working on
features to make it easier to implement backup correctly. So here you see my
podcasting app, again. And we have these two buttons--
take backup and restore backup now. And so if I click
on take backup, I now have the option to
choose the kind of backup type I want and pick a file name.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KXKP2tDPW4Y
text:
So here you see my
podcasting app, again. And we have these two buttons--
take backup and restore backup now. And so if I click
on take backup, I now have the option to
choose the kind of backup type I want and pick a file name. And when I do that, it's a
lot like taking an emulator snapshot. It's going to grab a
local file, and this is a file you would then put
in the project, typically, you can check it in. You can see I've already taken
several backups in the past here. And so I can now
restore these backups. So notice, again, how I have
no podcast subscriptions in my App State right now. But I'm going to restore a
backup I took when I did. So I just right click
and say restore app data. This takes about ten
seconds because it's not a very big backup. I had one where I
had also downloaded lots and lots of episodes. That was a 500 megabyte
backup, took longer. All right. So it's now pushed the
settings from my backup. And if I open this, it's as
if I had transferred my phone. And you can see I now
have all my subscriptions, along with some custom
filters, and even some listening statistics. So with this feature, I
can now go and make sure that my app is correctly
persisting and restoring data. And if not, I can debug it. And the way I would do that is
using the Run configuration. Under Run configurations, you
can click to restore app data and pick a backup file. So this is not just good for
users, this is good for you. Imagine how much easier
it is to clone your app settings across all your
AVDs or testing devices, than to each time log in. So hopefully, that will
make it a lot easier. And this is for
manual verification. We're also working on making
it easier to actually do this automatically through tests. And, of course, we're going
to support this for journeys, as well. But here, I'm showing you
the instrumentation test we're working on. So this is a special
restore test, right? So basically, I can point to
one of these backup files. It'll restore from that
backup before it runs to test.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KXKP2tDPW4Y
text:
But here, I'm showing you
the instrumentation test we're working on. So this is a special
restore test, right? So basically, I can point to
one of these backup files. It'll restore from that
